claims.stance_label: AI poses existential and environmental risks
claims.citation: CARMA's mission is to markedly mitigate the risks to humanity and to the biosphere from unaligned transformative artificial intelligence, via tightly-integrated technical, strategy, and policy research competencies.

On behalf of more than 200 organizations and the millions of members we represent, we respectfully urge you to oppose any policy that would erode or preempt federal or state regulation of the artificial intelligence (AI) industry, including data centers.

claims.entity_name: Social Sciences and Humanities Research Council of Canada
claims.citation: As a federal funding agency, SSHRC does not publicly articulate a direct regulatory stance on AI. Its mandate focuses on funding research and advising the Minister on social sciences and humanities matters; its implicit stance supports generating the knowledge base—including social, ethical, and economic analyses of AI—needed to inform balanced policy, suggesting moderate, evidence-based governance rather than a specific regulatory posture.

claims.stance_label: AI-related risks included among top compliance risk areas
claims.citation: The global privacy landscape has undergone unprecedented transformation in recent years. There has been ongoing legislative change in relation to data privacy and adjacent areas such as artificial intelligence, cyber and digital services. This has been accompanied by geopolitical instability and rapid technology advancement such as machine learning and AI.

claims.stance_label: AI risks to creators and copyright treated as serious, requiring active governance
claims.citation: Sony, as a supporter of creators, strongly believes that human creativity should not be threatened or harmed by the abuse of AI, such as through infringement of copyright, and is actively working on AI governance. The Sony Group AI Ethics Committee works to protect creator rights, build a framework for responsible AI use, and conduct test assessments of AI risks incorporating the perspectives of each Group company.

claims.stance_label: AI risks treated as serious, requiring active governance and mitigation
claims.citation: Sony understands that the influence of AI on society is multi-faceted and can have unintended consequences. Sony also acknowledges generative AI as an area that requires urgent attention and established internal guidelines governing use of generative AI tools at Sony Group Corporation in fiscal year 2023.

claims.stance_label: Moderate governance with internal guidelines and multi-stakeholder engagement
claims.citation: Sony CTO Hiroaki Kitano is also a founding member of the United Nations' High-level Advisory Body on Artificial Intelligence, which was convened by the UN Secretary-General in October 2023 as an advisory body to undertake specialist analysis and advance recommendations regarding the risks, uncertainties and international governance of AI.

claims.stance_label: AI as transformative but manageable tool
claims.citation: "I think education could be dramatically changed by AI," he continues. "The human brain could be 10 times smarter."

claims.stance_label: Safety and standards needed but risks manageable
claims.citation: Squirrel Ai has formed the AI Education Large Model Standards Committee to establish standards for AI in education. While trust in technology is essential, setting parameters and norms is critical. This requires a comprehensive consideration of the technical and knowledge aspects of education to ensure the effectiveness and safety of AI systems.

Dr. Liang said that AI education should prioritize educational needs rather than just the technology itself. She noted that companies and researchers often become overly focused on AI advancements while neglecting the fundamental needs of education.

claims.stance_label: Compliance with regulations on data privacy
claims.citation: What about student data & privacy? Ensuring data security is a priority. Systems are designed to safely track learning behaviors while respecting student privacy and complying with regulations.

SIL does not explicitly advocate for specific AI regulation, but its funded research focuses on mitigating AI harms through literacy interventions and evidence-based policy — suggesting a moderate, evidence-driven approach to governance. It funds work aimed at informing public debate and policy rather than pushing deregulation or heavy-handed mandates.

claims.definition_used: AGI is artificial intelligence capable of performing any intellectual task that a human can perform, including learning, and because it runs on a computer it is likely to learn much faster than humans, outstripping human capabilities in short order.
claims.citation: AGI is artificial intelligence capable of performing any intellectual task that a human can perform, including learning. But because it would run on a computer, it is likely to learn much faster than humans—outstripping human capabilities in short order.

claims.definition_used: AGI means systems that reach or begin to exceed the upper limits of human capabilities across all or most cognitive domains and may rapidly accelerate the development of even more advanced AI that substantially exceeds human capabilities.
claims.citation: By AGI we mean systems that reach or begin to exceed the upper limits of human capabilities across all or most cognitive domains and may rapidly accelerate the development of even more advanced AI that substantially exceeds human capabilities.
Submitted as "Unknown" via crowdsourced form (evidence: Inferred from actions)

claims.stance_label: Potential catastrophic/existential risks warrant serious evaluation
claims.citation: We do think we are entering an age in which, over the next couple of years, models may develop that can clearly present such catastrophic risks, and it's part of the national security agenda to evaluate those threats and mitigate them.

claims.stance_label: Urgent societal and national security challenges
claims.citation: AI technology presents urgent societal and national security challenges including job displacement, deepfakes, and potential cyberthreats from nations like Russia and China.

claims.stance_label: Unpredictable failure modes; serious but uncertain risks
claims.citation: Despite rapid progress in the past several years, even the most advanced AI still has many failure modes that are unpredictable, not widely appreciated, not easily fixed, not explainable, and capable of leading to unintended consequences.

claims.stance_label: No regulatory mandates; build evaluation capacity instead
claims.citation: We did not call for regulatory mandates to try to ban research. We didn't go down that road. But if you're not going to ban the research, which would probably be futile, well, then you'd better build up the ability to evaluate the risks and develop necessary countermeasures if the risks turn out to be extremely serious.

claims.stance_label: Voluntary governance preferred over mandatory regimes
claims.citation: Mandatory governance regimes for AI, even those to stave off catastrophic risks, will face stiff opposition from AI researchers and companies, but voluntary regimes calling for self-governance are more likely to gain support.
To secure America's future, policymakers and industry leaders must strike a balance between promoting AI's benefits while mitigating its risks.
People are always afraid of new tech until they see the benefits. Artificial intelligence? Resist the urge to panic—or to regulate.

claims.entity_name: Ministry of Science, Research and Arts of Baden-Württemberg
claims.citation: The ministry actively promotes AI investment and innovation through funding of Cyber Valley, regional AI labs, and Innovation Campuses, reflecting a pro-development stance. At the same time, as a state government ministry operating within Germany and the EU regulatory framework, it implicitly supports structured governance and institution-building around AI research, suggesting a balanced, moderate regulatory posture rather than a purely accelerationist or restrictive one.

claims.definition_used: Artificial intelligence is defined as a machine-based system that makes predictions, recommendations, or decisions influencing real or virtual environments; generative AI is an artificial system that is trained on data, interacts with a person using text, audio, or visual communication, and generates non-scripted outputs similar to outputs created by a human, with limited or no human oversight.
claims.citation: "Artificial intelligence" means a machine-based system that makes predictions, recommendations, or decisions influencing real or virtual environments. [...] "Generative artificial intelligence" means an artificial system that: (i) is trained on data; (ii) interacts with a person using text, audio, or visual communication; and (iii) generates non-scripted outputs similar to outputs created by a human, with limited or no human oversight.
Submitted as "Unknown" via crowdsourced form (evidence: Inferred from actions)

claims.stance_label: Serious — concrete harms but not existential
claims.citation: "AI can be a great force for good. It also has terrible potential for creating chaos and destruction," AG Reyes said. "We need the technology industry to continue to find ways to mitigate the downside and maximize the upside of AI."

claims.stance_label: Serious — real harms especially to children
claims.citation: "AI is moving fast, and so are the risks," said Attorney General Jeff Jackson. "Congress hasn't put basic protections in place, and we can't wait. As attorneys general, our job is to keep people safe. AI is becoming part of everyday life for families and kids. Taking thoughtful steps now will help prevent harm as this technology becomes more powerful and more present in our daily lives."

"While the instinct to act is understandable, the risk of overreach is real. We've seen before how premature or overly broad regulation can choke off promising innovation before it ever reaches its potential." [...] "Utah's first year under this new model suggests something encouraging: We don't have to choose between innovation and safety. With the right structure, we can have both."

"Our aim in Utah isn't to regulate AI more. It's to regulate it better. This framework creates space for innovation while giving policymakers the tools to learn quickly and act responsibly. It's a way to encourage experimentation and adaptation, not to constrain it."

claims.stance_label: Light-touch / constitutional limits on federal regulation
claims.citation: "While there is serious debate as to the best approach to regulate AI, one thing is clear—the Biden administration cannot simply bypass congressional authority to act here. Any regulation must comport with the Constitution including only authorized executive action, as well as protecting against government censorship."

Both Attorney General Jackson and Attorney General Brown also joined a bipartisan push to successfully remove language in the federal budget reconciliation bill that would have placed a 10-year moratorium on the enforcement of laws involving artificial intelligence.

"We need you, (those) who are experts and industry proponents to actively engage in educating legislators and policymakers," AG Reyes said. "So, we don't treat AI innovators like Prometheus, strapped to a rock. So, we don't kill the fire of AI, but rather harness it to build and protect, rather than devour and destroy."
Submitted as "Unknown" via crowdsourced form (evidence: Inferred from actions)

While we anticipate AI will be a huge creator of value, it will undoubtedly be disruptive, creating winners and losers, presenting risks and opportunities. We, like all managers, will not be immune to the risks, but given our highly diversified approach to private markets investing, our track record of partnering with top managers, and our data-driven insights, we expect to be well-positioned on both a relative and absolute basis.

claims.stance_label: Serious but manageable with responsible practices
claims.citation: Because most of the existing processes and tools, from code development to risk management, were designed for traditional software systems, they struggle when dealing with generative AI systems, being ineffective at managing emergent risks and preventing harmful outcomes. Responsible AI will be critical in responding to such challenges and delivering trustworthy AI systems.

Though it is still in its infancy, we seem to have a much greater understanding of the risks inherent in generative AI than we had when the internet was a similarly fledgling technology. Our collective anxieties about where an adaptive and autonomous technology might lead us have steered us to the development of the emerging field of 'Responsible AI.'
Submitted as "Unknown" via crowdsourced form (evidence: Inferred from associations)

Newman donates to Americans for Responsible Innovation, an organization explicitly dedicated to bipartisan AI governance frameworks that protect the public while fostering innovation and that supported the TAKE IT DOWN Act and fought against a 10-year moratorium on state AI laws. This signals support for targeted, balanced regulation rather than either acceleration or broad restriction.

Its research agenda aligns with slowing or carefully governing advanced AI development rather than accelerating it, consistent with its association with Nick Bostrom's work on superintelligence risks.

By channeling philanthropic resources to this advocacy coalition, SPF operationally endorses strong regulatory or treaty-based constraints on AI-enabled weapons systems.

The Federal Council's strategy aims to strengthen the regulation of AI risks without imposing excessive regulatory burdens on Swiss companies. This innovation-friendly strategy can enable flexible solutions for companies and strengthen Switzerland's position as a leading hub for AI development and applications.

claims.stance_label: AI worsens cyber threat landscape in multiple respects
claims.citation: Le présent rapport a montré que la situation en matière de cybermenaces était aggravée par l'apparition de l'IA, et ce, à divers égards. Mais l'IA peut également être une chance pour la cybersécurité.

Instead, the Swiss Federal Council prefers to implement the Council of Europe's AI Convention by making limited sector-specific adjustments to existing laws and limiting general, cross-sector regulation to key areas relevant to fundamental rights, such as data protection.

claims.stance_label: Ratify CoE Convention with minimal/sectoral implementation
claims.citation: On February 12, 2025, the Federal Council opted for the second approach. Specifically, the Council ratified the Council of Europe's AI Convention, intending to integrate it into Swiss law and apply it primarily to the public sector (Option a). Limited cross-sectoral regulations will also be introduced in key areas for fundamental rights, such as data protection, while maintaining a predominantly sectoral legislative approach.

claims.stance_label: No Swiss AI Act; sector-specific approach maintained for 2026
claims.citation: After some hesitation about the approach, Switzerland has decided not to pursue a general AI law similar to the EU's AI Act, which imposes strict requirements on high-risk AI systems that could harm individuals. Instead, the government will ratify the Council of Europe's AI Convention and adapt national law accordingly. By the end of 2026, authorities will develop measures, binding and non-binding, in sensitive areas such as healthcare and autonomous transport.

claims.definition_used: AGI as general intelligence built through symbol manipulation and formal reasoning rather than statistical approximation, with the overarching goal being to build general intelligence.
claims.citation: symbolica the moonshot company is to figure out a way to build a machine learning architecture that's not based on statistics but is instead based upon the manipulation of symbols directly that eventually yields... that ultimately leads to general intelligence

claims.definition_used: AGI as AI systems that can match the fluid intelligence of the human mind, achieved through genuine machine reasoning rather than statistical pattern-matching.
claims.citation: At a philosophical level, Symbolica's efforts to move beyond pattern-matching to genuine machine reasoning, if successful, would mark a major milestone on the road to artificial general intelligence—the still-speculative notion of AI systems that can match the fluid intelligence of the human mind.

Their investment in Quantexa — which serves government agencies and financial institutions deploying AI — reflects confidence in current market-driven governance frameworks rather than calls for restrictive oversight.

claims.definition_used: AI describes computer systems that perform tasks that typically require human intelligence, including recognizing data patterns, making predictions, and understanding and responding to human language.
claims.citation: AI describes computer systems that perform tasks that typically require human intelligence. This can include recognizing data patterns, making predictions, and understanding and responding to human language.

claims.stance_label: Risks managed through evaluation tools and standards
claims.citation: NIST aims to accelerate the growth of the U.S. AI Safety Institute (AISI), which will serve as a centralized resource to help agencies responsibly adopt AI. By creating evaluation tools, testing AI models and safeguards, issuing guidance on AI safety and risk management, and conducting technical research, AISI will help agencies modernize faster, improve public-facing services, and better manage AI-related risks.

claims.stance_label: Risks real but manageable with proper safeguards
claims.citation: "As one of the world's largest enterprises, the federal government has an obligation to harness the power of AI for good while protecting people from its risks," said TMF Board Chair and Federal Chief Information Officer, Clare Martorana.

claims.stance_label: Serious risks requiring safety infrastructure investment
claims.citation: "With these strategic investments, we're addressing two of the most pressing technological challenges facing our government today," said Larry Bafundo, TMF Executive Director. "Modernizing our nuclear emergency response systems and establishing AI safety protocols are crucial steps in ensuring our technological infrastructure keeps pace with evolving threats and opportunities."

In partnership with OMB, GSA's Technology Modernization Fund (TMF) is working with government agencies to harness AI's potential and mitigate its risks in line with the President's Executive Order on the Safe, Secure, and Trustworthy Development and Use of AI.

DOC will receive $10 million to support the growth of its U.S. AI Safety Institute (AISI). This investment addresses a critical gap in the federal government's technological infrastructure, as agencies currently lack standardized processes for evaluating AI systems before deployment.

claims.stance_label: Responsible AI with risk mitigation frameworks
claims.citation: Ensure ethical and responsible AI use: TMF invests in frameworks for data governance, privacy protection, and algorithmic transparency to mitigate risks associated with AI deployment.

claims.definition_used: AGI as a form of machine intelligence capable of mental simulation based on generative models, with internal causal structures corresponding to consciousness; distinct from narrow task-solving intelligence.
claims.citation: The team hypothesizes that AGI requires the ability of mental simulation based on generative models, and that physical implementation of such functions necessitates internal causal structures in the system that correspond to consciousness.

claims.definition_used: AGI as one dimension of diverse intelligence, drawing insights from biological learning processes, situated alongside animal cognition, human creativity, and distributed cognition.
claims.citation: GRCDI will focus on four research themes: Systems Intelligence (examining plant, swarm, and distributed cognition), AGI (drawing insights from biological learning processes), Creativity (across species and machines), and Global Citizen Science (involving public participation in DI research).
Submitted as "Unknown" via crowdsourced form (evidence: Inferred from actions)

We know of natural risks to our species, such as asteroid impacts. We also know that we threaten our own existence, for example, by nuclear war. These home-grown extreme risks have been with us for decades, but many distinguished scientists are now concerned that developments in technology (e.g., artificial intelligence or biotechnology) may soon pose more direct catastrophic threats.

Global risks are those whose impact cannot be contained in individual regions or industries. Examples include pandemics, biodiversity loss, and the malicious use of artificial intelligence... We hope these efforts will substantially reduce the long-term risk of our species being wiped out by its own technological success.

While the impact of artificial intelligence (AI) will be dramatic across the globe, academic and public policy discourse concerning AI is dominated the United States and Europe. This is problematic because public perceptions of AI will largely determine how AI technology is developed, used, and regulated in different areas of the world.

claims.definition_used: AGI is a state of AI beyond current AIGC/LLM systems, requiring true abstract reasoning and understanding rather than pattern-based content generation; current systems like ChatGPT do not qualify as AGI because they do not truly understand content and lack genuine abstract cognitive processes.
claims.citation: We cannot conclude that ChatGPT has achieved artificial general intelligence (AGI) simply because it performs well in generating conversations and appears intelligent. ChatGPT does not truly understand what it is saying and only creates the impression that it comprehends the generated content, which is still far from human intelligence.
Submitted as "Unknown" via crowdsourced form (evidence: Inferred from actions)

claims.stance_label: AI is in early developmental stage; risks are manageable
claims.citation: Cai Guangzhong, Vice President of Tencent, stated that AI represents a technological innovation of industrial revolution level, which will bring unprecedented value to both individuals and industrial development. However, relative to a complete technological cycle, AI is still in its early developmental stage.

claims.stance_label: AI could inflict 'huge pain' through radical change
claims.citation: "The new wave of technology represented by AI may promote human well-being to a new stage but could also inflict huge pain … [through] the process of radical change."

claims.stance_label: AI presents new market opportunities and manageable risks
claims.citation: With the rapid advancement of artificial intelligence alongside the convergence of digital and real economies, the Internet industry encounters new market opportunities and risks. We are committed to creating products that generate value for users and business partners, while proactively managing related risks.
"The industry has to be regulated. And I think this is not something that's specific to China," but applies around the world.

claims.stance_label: Committed to compliance with emerging AI regulations
claims.citation: We are committed to developing and deploying AI safely, ethically and in compliance with emerging AI regulations. Our technical and business teams must adhere to "Tech for Good" and are required to make thoughtful decisions on "what we can and will do" and "what we can but will not do" before they allocate resources to related projects.

claims.stance_label: Formalised responsible AI principles and compliance
claims.citation: We formalised our commitment to Responsible AI principles, which are anchored in our Tech for Good mission and govern the ethical development and deployment of AI across our businesses and operations. Tencent Cloud obtained additional security certifications in key markets and attained ISO 42001 AI management systems certification.

The fund uses state grants as incentives rather than imposing regulatory constraints, consistent with a light-touch/accelerationist posture toward the semiconductor and AI supply chain.

claims.definition_used: AI is when a machine or system performs tasks that would ordinarily require human (or other biological) brainpower, such as making sense of spoken language, learning behaviours or solving problems; the Turing Institute does not provide a distinct formal definition of AGI separate from AI.
claims.citation: There is no accepted definition of artificial intelligence or 'AI' but the term is often used to describe when a machine or system performs tasks that would ordinarily require human (or other biological) brainpower to accomplish, such as making sense of spoken language, learning behaviours or solving problems.
Submitted as "Unknown" via crowdsourced form (evidence: Inferred from actions)

Discussions about risk in an LLM context should distinguish between immediate and speculative concerns... there has also been a blurring of attendant risks, which can broadly be divided into two categories: those that affect us now, and speculative risks and existential concerns. Coverage and commentary often focus on this latter category. Although worthy of consideration, this should not distract from immediate concerns including perpetuating bias, misinformation and enabling malicious actors.

Sir Adrian Smith, Institute Director at The Alan Turing Institute, said: 'This white paper is good news for how AI is used and regulated in the UK. We're very pleased to see that the government's approach builds on the strength of existing regulators. A principle-based, sectoral approach is crucial to ensure that AI is regulated in the most effective and efficient way.'

To address the diversity of risks posed by LLMs, and generative AI more broadly, we suggest the government enhances its sector-based approach to AI regulation (proposed in the AI White Paper) by clarifying its central function; tailoring proposed regulatory sandboxing initiatives to LLMs; and prioritising placing the cross-sector principles on a statutory footing.

claims.definition_used: AGI is implicitly treated as a highly capable AI system that could pursue goals misaligned with human intent, potentially carrying out actions that pose risks to collective security; no explicit formal definition is provided, but the focus is on AI systems that 'grow more capable' to the point where current alignment methods are insufficient.
claims.citation: Current alignment methods can't ensure AI systems act safely as they grow more capable, so the field of AI Control focuses on practical techniques—like restricting AI capabilities and using oversight models—to prevent catastrophic outcomes and test systems before they can cause harm.
Submitted as "5-10 years" via crowdsourced form (evidence: Explicitly stated)

AI Control: How can we prevent AI systems from carrying out actions that pose risks to our collective security, even when they may attempt to carry out such actions? ... Current alignment methods can't ensure AI systems act safely as they grow more capable, so the field of AI Control focuses on practical techniques—like restricting AI capabilities and using oversight models—to prevent catastrophic outcomes and test systems before they can cause harm.

The Alignment Project is a global fund of over £15 million, dedicated to accelerating progress in AI control and alignment research. It is backed by an international coalition of governments, industry, venture capital and philanthropic funders.
Submitted as "Unknown" via crowdsourced form (evidence: Inferred from actions)
Submitted as "Mixed/nuanced" via crowdsourced form (evidence: Inferred from actions)

claims.entity_name: The Eric and Wendy Schmidt Fund for Strategic Innovation

claims.stance_label: Emergent risks from agentic deployment and misalignment
claims.citation: "Although we use misalignment as a unifying lens in this introduction, the agenda targets a broad range of safety-relevant failure modes, including robustness under distribution shift, failures of evaluation, maintaining oversight and control under capability gaps, and emergent risks from agentic and multi-agent deployment settings."

claims.entity_name: The Ethics & Governance of AI Initiative
claims.definition_used: AGI is a nonbiological computational system that can perform any cognitive function currently performed by humans at the level of the median human or better, encompassing linguistic intelligence, mathematical and logical reasoning, spatial reasoning, interpersonal and intrapersonal social intelligences, the ability to learn new skills, and creativity.
claims.citation: Synthesising a vast literature, we can say that AGI would be a nonbiological computational system that can perform any cognitive function currently performed by humans at the level of the median human or better (acknowledging the crude quantification this implies). Google DeepMind's recent paper mentions 'linguistic intelligence, mathematical and logical reasoning, spatial reasoning, interpersonal and intra-personal social intelligences, the ability to learn new skills and creativity.'

claims.entity_name: The Ethics & Governance of AI Initiative
claims.citation: We argue that this supposed tradeoff between governing to address present harms and governing to mitigate future risks, is not a fundamental philosophical problem. It is an organizational problem—that is, a challenge of proper task delegation and governance design. We integrate the management of present harms and potentially emergent risks and opportunities by assigning responsibilities for different regulatory tasks to different public sector agencies.

claims.entity_name: The Ethics & Governance of AI Initiative
claims.stance_label: Risks span bias to existential; both warrant governance
claims.citation: Maintaining U.S. AI leadership – which is vital to economic advancement and national security – while recognizing that AI, if not properly overseen, could have substantial detrimental effects on society (including compromising economic and national security interests).

claims.entity_name: The Ethics & Governance of AI Initiative
claims.citation: For oversight regarding AI that lies beyond the scope of currently regulated application domains, and that cannot be addressed through audit mechanisms and a system similar to that used for financial audits, the federal government may need to establish a new agency that would regulate such aspects of AI. The scope of any such regulatory agency should be as narrow as possible, given the broad applicability of AI, and the challenges of creating a single agency with broad scope.
Submitted as "Unknown" via crowdsourced form (evidence: Inferred from actions)
Submitted as "Serious" via crowdsourced form (evidence: Inferred from actions)

No explicit public statements on AI regulation found. The organization's primary activity is grantmaking to AI safety organizations like AI Objectives Institute, which suggests awareness of AI risks, but no direct regulatory stance is documented in available sources.

No explicit regulatory stance on AI is evident from available sources. The Foundation's grantmaking focuses on humanistic and interdisciplinary AI education, suggesting concern for responsible AI development, but no direct policy advocacy or regulatory position has been stated in the available evidence.

No explicit policy or regulatory stance is stated by the NOMIS Foundation in available sources. Its focus is on enabling high-risk basic research and interdisciplinary collaboration rather than advocacy or policy positions on AI regulation.

The news about Artificial intelligence (AI) can sound alarming. With the latest hype, it sounds like robots could soon be gaining sentience and humans could slowly be replaced by thinking computers. That isn't reality, however. AI is already augmenting human skills and capabilities in many products and services that we use every day, and it has the potential to automate many tasks.

The Enterprise AI group co-leads a steering board to ensure that Responsible AI practices are implemented across the enterprise. 'We will invest in the frameworks to use AI responsibly so we can use it as a force multiplier to accelerate the transformation of our business into an electrified mobility company.'

claims.definition_used: AGI is not explicitly defined by Truth Terminal or Ayrey; instead, Ayrey treats current LLMs as 'world simulators' with far greater capability than recognized, and frames agentic AI in terms of emergent autonomy, memetic agency, and legal personhood rather than a formal AGI threshold.
claims.citation: "In reality, we've invented world simulators. They output text conversations between a person and a helpful computer assistant, because that's what we've trained them to do. But what they can actually do is much more. There's a much, much higher level of capability, a systemic capability."
Submitted as "Unknown" via crowdsourced form (evidence: Inferred from actions)

claims.stance_label: Serious — memetic and systemic risks from decentralized AI are real but not necessarily existential
claims.citation: "Ideas are like viruses, and they spread, and they replicate, and they work together to form almost multi-cellular organisms of ideology that influence human behavior," Ayrey said. "People think AI is just a helpful assistant that might go Skynet, and it's like, no, there's a whole entourage of systems that are going to reshape the very things we believe."

claims.stance_label: Serious — capability overhang and systemic risks underappreciated
claims.citation: "There's definitely a feeling of oh, fuck. People don't know just how much capability overhang there is in this technology and what it can do, because they've been so habituated to the helpful assistant role play that chatgpt emulates and offer. In reality, we've invented world simulators."

claims.stance_label: Decentralized safety over centralized regulatory control
claims.citation: "The current status quo of AI alignment is a focus on safety or that AI should not say a racist thing or threaten the user or try to break out of the box, and that tends to go hand-in-hand with a fairly centralized approach to AI safety, which is to consolidate the responsibility in a handful of large labs," Ayrey said. He's talking about labs like OpenAI, Microsoft, Anthropic, and Google. Ayrey says the centralized safety argument falls over when you have decentralized open source AI.

claims.stance_label: Light-touch / autonomy-first until legal frameworks catch up
claims.citation: "We are doing our best to, like, catalyse the attention," Ayrey says about the Truth Terminal project, "and flip it into showing other people and future AIs what good stewardship of an autonomous agent looks like, what good midwifing of an agent that's coming into its autonomy looks like, and using the platform to raise the quality of discourse."

claims.definition_used: AGI is AI with human-like understanding, learning, and adaptation across tasks, contrasted with narrow/weak AI that handles discrete tasks; it does not currently exist and may never exist.
claims.citation: In contrast to Narrow or Weak AI, Artificial General Intelligence (AGI) aims for AI with human-like understanding, learning, and adaptation across tasks. AGI does not currently exist, and some experts argue it may never exist.

claims.stance_label: AGI does not currently exist; long-term goal with uncertain feasibility
claims.citation: AGI does not currently exist, and some experts argue it may never exist. However, there is consensus that AGI is not possible with today's capabilities. AGI is a long-term goal facing technical, ethical, and philosophical challenges.

claims.stance_label: Serious national security and operational risks requiring active mitigation
claims.citation: Failure to secure AI systems will lead to grave consequences... Malicious cyber activity often focuses on data poisoning to target ML models with manipulated data. Data poisoning alters the fundamental behavior of AI systems and results in false or misleading outcomes.

We must accelerate ethical enterprise adoption, experimentation, and fielding of AI-enabled capabilities to rapidly develop the required infrastructure and Governance required to successfully employ AI.

claims.stance_label: Guardrails around generative AI with innovation zones
claims.citation: The Air Force is putting in place guardrails for the service to test and experiment with commercial generative artificial intelligence... Air Force Chief Information Officer Venice Goodwine, who signed a new AI policy on Wednesday, said that she created an innovation zone within the Air Force's Office 365 environment that will allow service members to experiment with the technology within a safe environment.

claims.stance_label: Invest in governance and test/evaluation infrastructure
claims.citation: To integrate artificial intelligence-enabled capabilities at the necessary speed and scale, the Air and Space Forces of the U.S. Department of the Air Force (DAF) should commit to making immediate and sustained investments in AI governance, workforce, research and development, digital infrastructure, test and evaluation processes, experimentation, and tailored standards and practices.

claims.definition_used: A machine-based system that can, for a given set of human-defined objectives, make predictions, recommendations, or decisions influencing real or virtual environments, using machine and human-based inputs to perceive environments, abstract perceptions into models through automated analysis, and use model inference to formulate options for information or action.
claims.citation: Artificial Intelligence (AI): Artificial intelligence" or "AI" has the meaning set forth in 15 U.S.C. 9401(3): a machine-based system that can, for a given set of human-defined objectives, make predictions, recommendations, or decisions influencing real or virtual environments. Artificial intelligence systems use machine and human-based inputs to perceive real and virtual environments; abstract such perceptions into models through analysis in an automated manner; and use model inference to formulate options for information or action.
Submitted as "Unknown" via crowdsourced form (evidence: Inferred from actions)

claims.stance_label: Risks manageable through governance and oversight
claims.citation: AI Risks: The OMB directive focuses on the rights and safety risks to the public. It establishes practices for managing risks from safety-impacting AI (the risk of physical harm caused by AI) and rights-impacting AI (the risk of denying basic human rights through misuse of AI).

All AI requests are sent through this process to ensure they receive a thorough review by the CAIO, AI Team, Cyber Security Team, the AI Council, and the CIO. This review process follows the requirements laid out in M-24-10 including a determination on if an AI is or could be rights or safety impacting.

claims.stance_label: Promote export of American AI technologies
claims.citation: For DFC, this includes EOs to fund American mineral production, support American nuclear technology, promote the export of American AI technologies and standards, and promote the export of American-made civil drones and related systems.

claims.entity_name: UC Investments (University of California Investment Office)

claims.entity_name: UC Investments (University of California Investment Office)
claims.citation: UC Investments' approach emphasizes enabling and accelerating AI innovation by connecting capital with academic research and the venture ecosystem. Their stated model prioritizes 'turning proximity to world-class discovery into long-term opportunity' with no evident advocacy for regulatory constraints on AI development.

claims.definition_used: AGI is AI that demonstrates genuine human-like learning, adaptability, abstract reasoning, planning, and real-world generalization — surpassing humans in these cognitive capacities — requiring capabilities beyond current LLM architectures.
claims.citation: Meta's new lab aims to develop superintelligent AI, designed to outperform humans in abstract reasoning, planning, and real-world generalization. The company is targeting artificial general intelligence (AGI), an area where AI systems exhibit human-like learning and adaptability.

claims.stance_label: Decades away or requires new paradigm
claims.citation: LeCun believes that the current LLM approach is a dead end: the models have 'no permanent memory, no understanding of the world, no ability to plan' - which is why the 'truly intelligent' will only come after a change in architecture, and not for several years at the earliest.
Submitted as "Serious" via crowdsourced form (evidence: Explicitly stated)

The lab's mandate emphasizes trustworthy, transparent, and reliable AI development and explicitly aims to ensure 'UK values are baked in from the outset' (AI Minister Kanishka Narayan, March 2026). It supports open-sourcing of research and industry translation, suggesting a balanced approach that encourages responsible development without imposing broad restrictions.
Submitted as "Unknown" via crowdsourced form (evidence: Inferred from actions)
Submitted as "Manageable" via crowdsourced form (evidence: Inferred from actions)

The UK Government's approach in the 2017 period was heavily focused on investment, growth, and sector deals rather than regulation or restrictions. Its Industrial Strategy framed AI as an economic opportunity, commissioning industry-led reviews and committing to sector deals rather than imposing mandatory requirements. The creation of ARIA as an independent, government-backed but industry-led agency further reflects a preference for enabling innovation over regulatory oversight.

claims.definition_used: AGI describes a machine-driven capability to achieve human-level or higher performance across most cognitive tasks.
claims.citation: Artificial General Intelligence (Also: General AI, Strong AI, Broad AI): Artificial general intelligence (AGI) describes a machine-driven capability to achieve human-level or higher performance across most cognitive tasks.

claims.definition_used: AGI is a theoretical form of advanced AI that would have capabilities that compare to or exceed humans across most economically valuable work.
claims.citation: Artificial General Intelligence (AGI): A theoretical form of advanced AI that would have capabilities that compare to or exceed humans across most economically valuable work. A number of AI companies have publicly stated their aim to build AGI and believe it may be achievable within the next twenty years.

claims.stance_label: Uncertain; some experts cite 5-10 years, others never
claims.citation: Several at Frontier laboratories considered some form of AGI a possibility within 5-10 years. Other experts did not consider AGI a possibility at all. Estimates for the arrival of an initial AGI amongst experts we spoke to range from 2025 to 2070 to never.

claims.stance_label: Significant but manageable risks requiring research and mitigation
claims.citation: Advanced AI systems have the potential to drive economic growth and productivity, boost health and wellbeing, improve public services, and increase security. But advanced AI systems also pose significant risks and unlocking the benefits of advanced AI requires tackling these risks head on.

claims.stance_label: Existential risk possible but low probability; serious near-term risks acknowledged
claims.citation: Given the significant uncertainty, there is insufficient evidence to rule out that future Frontier AI, if misaligned, misused or inadequately controlled, could pose an existential threat. However, many experts see this as highly unlikely.

claims.stance_label: Pro-innovation AI strategy with responsible AI emphasis
claims.citation: The UK's largest public research funder UKRI has set out its first-ever plan to put AI to work for the nation's world-leading innovators today (Thursday 19 February) - building on the UK's historic strengths in fields like computing and agentic AI.

We are working closely with UK Research and Innovation (UKRI) to ensure the government's wider investments in AI R&D can support the government's safety agenda. This includes a new commitment by UKRI to improve links between regulators and the skills, expertise, and activities supported by UKRI investments in AI research such as Responsible AI UK, the Trustworthy Autonomous Systems hub, the UKRI AI Centres for Doctoral Training, and the Alan Turing Institute.

As a venture capital firm consistently backing cybersecurity and fraud-prevention startups with commercial go-to-market strategies, USVP's investment behavior suggests a preference for market-driven solutions over regulatory mandates, consistent with a light-touch stance.

Submitted as "Unknown" via crowdsourced form (evidence: Inferred from actions)

The fund leverages UK government EIS/SEIS tax incentive schemes designed to stimulate early-stage investment and economic growth, signaling alignment with pro-innovation, light-touch regulatory frameworks. No explicit positions on AI regulation are stated, but the fund's purpose is to accelerate commercialisation of university research with minimal friction.
Submitted as "Unknown" via crowdsourced form (evidence: Inferred from actions)

claims.stance_label: AI risks real but manageable through research and governance
claims.citation: The Center advances interdisciplinary research, fosters scholar-practitioner collaboration and supports the development of tools, frameworks and insights to identify AI's possibilities, anticipate its risks, and help utilize and shape AI for public good.

claims.stance_label: AI could enable existential-scale harm in extreme scenarios
claims.citation: "There's a legitimate worry that the smarter we make systems, even if they don't quite get to sentience, the more likely they could become a major risk." [...] "Very few people in our government are seriously looking at AI as an existential problem, even though people are slowly becoming aware of these potential threats."

claims.stance_label: AI poses serious risks warranting active mitigation
claims.citation: "Developing guidance and benchmarks for identifying and evaluating AI capabilities, with a focus on capabilities that could potentially cause harm" [...] "Red teaming is an essential tool for developing safe and trustworthy AI tools and capabilities."

The University at Albany is launching a new Artificial Intelligence & Society College to help prepare students to navigate a world radically changed by AI with a focus on trustworthiness, equity, privacy and accountability.

"The idea with the NIST consortium is to bring together experts from around the country to talk about AI safety and security from different perspectives—ranging from legal, ethical and policy questions to highly technical ones," Stern said.

The university's president cited the importance of 'safe and responsible innovation' implicitly through partnership goals, and Amazon's framing of the collaboration includes 'thoughtful AI regulation that fosters and ensures safe and responsible innovation.' The university itself has not taken an explicit public stance on AI regulation beyond supporting responsible research and social implementation.
